briefing.opening: Good morning. We will now begin the climate briefing for July 12, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Extreme Climate Disasters: Casualties and Infrastructure Threats
sections.content: Recently, extreme weather phenomena caused by climate change have led to severe casualties and threats to social infrastructure worldwide. Domestically, torrential rainfall caused extensive damage, with 44 fatalities in a single week and widespread flooding [1, 13]. In the Pohang and Gyeongsan regions, the first severe heatwave warning of the year was issued due to life-threatening high temperatures [10, 39, 42, 43]. Europe is also experiencing widespread damage from a ten-day heatwave reaching 42 degrees Celsius, including large-scale wildfires in Spain that killed 12 people and the closure of the Eiffel Tower [12, 14]. Direct impacts of climate change are evident across the international community, such as seawater intrusion in Gambia and a crisis of avian species loss in the UK [44, 47]. These situations raise fundamental questions about whether our infrastructure is prepared for the impacts of climate change [45, 46], and concerns are escalating with the possibility that a 'super' El Niño could shock global food prices by 2028 [49].

sections.title: 🌱 Expansion of Domestic Carbon Neutrality Education and Climate Response Cooperation
sections.content: Domestic and international educational and cooperative efforts to address the climate crisis are actively underway. The Wonju Climate Change Promotion Center is offering carbon neutrality education and children's experiential programs until September [2, 3, 5, 15]. Citizen participation is also being encouraged in Cheongju's Heungdeok-gu and the Bubal Youth Center, where outreach education and AI-powered carbon neutrality projects have been successfully completed [20, 21]. Internationally, South Korea and Mongolia are strengthening cooperation for desertification prevention and forest restoration [4, 7]. The Korea Insurance Development Institute is sharing AI and climate crisis response strategies with Malaysia [8]. Furthermore, various international movements are observed, such as MIKTA youths discussing climate change response and the official launch of Vietnam's carbon market [6, 23]. Domestically, Daejeon's Seo-gu district is practicing energy saving and sharing by donating carbon neutrality point incentives [18, 22, 24], and K-water continues its efforts to establish an RE100 ecosystem with hydrothermal energy, aiming to become a carbon-neutral hub in the central region [19].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Expansion of Renewable Energy and Grid Connection Challenges
sections.content: Efforts to expand renewable energy deployment are ongoing both domestically and internationally, but challenges related to infrastructure and grid connection are also emerging. Andong City is establishing a power supply system through renewable energy and customized support for businesses [27], and is expanding solar power deployment through an additional announcement for the 2026 Renewable Energy Housing and Building Support Project [28]. Notably, significant progress is being made in the offshore wind sector, with Korea Hydro & Nuclear Power (KHNP) and Hanwha Ocean jointly pursuing the 1,020MW Yeonggwang Chilhae Offshore Wind Project [33]. Additionally, construction has begun on power lines in Sinan and Mokpo, enhancing renewable energy grid connection and power supply stability [34, 35]. The Garden Project is exploring new possibilities by proposing ESG solutions for semiconductor and AI data centers that combine renewable energy with rainwater circulation [30, 36]. However, investor concerns are high regarding Australia's 82% renewable energy target [26], and solar grid bottlenecks in India threaten the achievement of its 2030 targets [38], further emphasizing the importance of renewable energy infrastructure development [32].

sections.title: ⚠️ Climate Policy Shift and Decarbonization Challenges in Healthcare and Industry
sections.content: Significant discussions and shifts are occurring regarding policy directions and the role of industry in responding to the climate crisis. The government has mentioned the need to consider constructing additional nuclear power plants to supply electricity to semiconductor and AI industrial complexes, signaling a potential shift from the existing 'nuclear phase-out' and 'nuclear reduction' stance to 'nuclear expansion' [37, 40]. This appears to be based on the judgment that renewable energy alone cannot meet industrial demand. Meanwhile, there is growing criticism that medical technologies, while saving lives, are paradoxically contributing to the climate crisis, leading to urgent calls for 'decarbonization' in the healthcare sector [11]. A situation has also been reported where Gwangju's carbon neutrality plan is being undermined by the central government's industrialization policies [17]. Furthermore, the electrical safety cooperation between KEPCO Imsil Branch and Imsil Nonghyup in preparation for heatwaves and torrential rains [9], along with international trends indicating that climate change behavioral science is under political attack [48], illustrate the complex challenges of addressing the climate crisis.

briefing.closing: As we have reviewed in today's briefing, we stand at the forefront of disasters caused by climate change, and this is a critical moment requiring decisive policy action and comprehensive societal efforts. This concludes today's climate briefing.

summary.phenomenon: On April 6, Minister Kim Sung-hwan of the Ministry of Climate, Energy, and Environment mentioned the necessity of reviewing additional nuclear power plant construction, citing the power supply for semiconductor and artificial intelligence (AI) industrial complexes.
summary.cause: The background for considering this policy shift is the judgment that the enormous power demand of semiconductor and artificial intelligence (AI) industrial complexes cannot be met by renewable energy alone.
summary.outlook: Concerns are being raised that the Ministry of Climate's renewable energy-centric energy transition policy may be retreating.

articles.original_description: Weather cycle threatens harvests worldwide, adding to inflation already fuelled by the Iran warEconomists are warning that a “super” El Niño weather cycle this year could cause a severe shock to global food prices lasting into 2028.As the Iran war pushes up world food prices to the highest level in three years, economists said supply chains faced “two shocks at once” stoked by extreme weather linked to global heating.
